revision.text:
[[File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja.jpg|thumb|right|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko jinja'''|吉備津彦神社|}}" , also known as '''Kibitsu-jinja'''<ref name="nussbaum513">[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "''Kibitsu-jinja''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref> or '''Kibitsuhiko Shrine''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsuhiko-jinja/ "Kibitsuhiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== ''Kitbitsuhiko jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bizen Province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
The shrine was built between 1390 and 1402.<ref name="nussbaum513"/>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitbitsuhiko-jinja}} * [http://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp/ Kibitsuhiko Shrine website {{ja icon}}]

(2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "''Kibitsu-jinja''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref> is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsuhiko-jinja/ "Kibitsuhiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== The shrine was built between 1390 and 1402.<ref name="nussbaum513"/>
''Kitbitsuhiko jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bizen Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
The shrine was one of 50 of the lowest ranked, nationally significant shrines ({{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社|}}).

revision.comment: Corrected years, which belonged to another shrine, [[Kibitsu Shrine]]. Removed ref about said other shrine
revision.text:
[[File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja.jpg|thumb|right|Kibitsuhiko Shrine]]
{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} '''Kibitsuhiko Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|吉備津彦神社|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}}), is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsuhiko-jinja/ "Kibitsuhiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== The inner shrine was rebuilt in 1697, while most other buildings were reconstructed in 1936 after a destructive fire in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kibitsuhiko Shrine |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/kibitsuhiko-shrine.html |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |accessdate=27 December 2017}}</ref>
Kitbitsuhiko Shrine is the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bizen Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]],<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of [[Emperor Korei]].<ref name="nussbaum513">{{cite encyclopedia|author-link=Louis-Frédéric |last=Nussbaum |first=Louis-Frédéric |date=2005 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= |title=Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto |encyclopedia=Japan Encyclopedia |p=513}}</ref>
The shrine is one of 50 national shrines of the lowest rank or {{nihongo|''[[Kokuhei Shōsha]]''|国幣小社|}}.
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} * [http://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp/ Kibitsuhiko Shrine website {{ja icon}}]
{{coord|34|40|36.2|N|133|51|49.9|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=title}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]]
{{Japan-struct-stub}}

The shrine is one of 50 national shrines of the lowest rank or {{nihongo|''[[Kokuhei Shōsha]]''|国幣小社|}}.

revision.text:
{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Shrine | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja.jpg | caption = Kibitsuhiko Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.2|N|133|51|49.9|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Okayama, Okayama]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Kibitsuhiko Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|吉備津彦神社|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}}), is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsuhiko-jinja/ "Kibitsuhiko Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== The inner shrine was rebuilt in 1697, while most other buildings were reconstructed in 1936 after a destructive fire in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kibitsuhiko Shrine |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/kibitsuhiko-shrine.html |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |accessdate=27 December 2017}}</ref>
Kitbitsuhiko Shrine is the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bizen Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]],<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of [[Emperor Korei]].<ref name="nussbaum513">{{cite encyclopedia|author-link=Louis-Frédéric |last=Nussbaum |first=Louis-Frédéric |date=2005 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= |title=Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto |encyclopedia=Japan Encyclopedia |p=513}}</ref>
The shrine is one of 50 national shrines of the lowest rank or {{nihongo|''[[Kokuhei Shōsha]]''|国幣小社|}}.
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} * [http://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp/ Kibitsuhiko Shrine website {{in lang|ja}}]
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]]
{{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conquerer of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} {{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:ibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]]

According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine|Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]]

According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine|Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Kosha]]

revision.comment: Disambiguating links to [[Kibitsu Shrine]] (link changed to [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)]]) using [[User:Qwertyytrewqqwerty/DisamAssist|DisamAssist]].
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken 701-1211 | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine|Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine|Ani Jinja]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]]

After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken 701-1211 | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead {{ill|Ani Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=安仁神社}} was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead {{ill|Ani Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=安仁神社}} was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Okayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu jinja (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsuhiko Jinja
吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken 701-1211 | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken 701-1211 | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

吉備津彦神社 | image = Kibitsuhiko-jinja tourou and haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden and Stone lantern of Kibitsuhiko Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|40|36.31|N|133|51|49.87|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsuhiko Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1043 Ichinomiya, Kita-ku, Okayama-shi, Okayama-ken 701-1211 | website = {{official|1=https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = third weekend of October | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko Jinja'''|吉備津彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bizen Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the third weekend of October. Theshrine is also known in literature as the {{nihongo|Asahi-no-miya|朝日の宮||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsuhiko Jinja are:{{fact|date=November 2022}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]''|吉備津彦命|}}, the son of [[Emperor Kōrei]] and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Wakatakehiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother or son of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōrei]]'''| 孝霊天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kōgen]]'''|孝元天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Kaika]]'''| 開化天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Emperor Sujin]]'''| 崇神天皇 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Hikosashikatawake-no-mikoto'''| 彦刺肩別命 |}}, brother of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Amatarashi-hiko Kunioshi-hito no mikoto'''|天足彦国押人命 |}} * {{nihongo|'''Yamato-totohimo-moso-hime-no-mikoto'''| 倭迹迹日百襲姫命 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato-totohiwakaya-hime-no-mikoto'''|大倭迹々日稚屋比売命 |}}, younger sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Kanayamahiko-no-kami'''|金山彦大神 |}}, elder sister of Kibitsuhiko * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamakuni-no-kami'''|大山咋神 |}} ==History== The origins of Kibitsuhiko Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's legend', it was during the reign of [[Empress Suiko]], and was the site of Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto's residence; but the shrine does not appear in any historical documentation until the late [[Heian period]]. It is not listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' and instead [[Ani Shrine]] was given the rank of {{nihongo|''Myojin Taisha''|名神大社||}} and the rank of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. However, Ani Jinja sided with [[Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] in his revolt from 939 to 941 and was thus demoted. On the other hand, [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]], the parent shrine of the Kibitsuhiko Jinja wrote prayers for the victory of imperial forces over Fujiwara no Sumitomo, and was thus rewarded by having their branch shrine in Bizen raised to become the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down by the [[Matsuda clan]], but was restored with the support of [[Ukita Naoie]]. [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] prayed here for victory prior to his attack on [[Takamatsu Castle (Bitchū)|Takamatsu Castle]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Ikeda Toshitaka]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Himeji Domain]] and the lord of [[Okayama Castle]], rebuilt the shrine, and successive rulers of [[Okayama Domain]] sponsored various repairs and reconstructions. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was listed as a Prefecture Shrine, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1928.In December 1930, the main shrine and most other structures (with the exception of the Zuijinmon Gate) burned down due to accident. The shrine buildings that can be seen today were completed in 1936.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is a three-minute walk from [[Bizen-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Kibi Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== *[[Tachi]] Japanese sword. [[Edo Period]], National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. Signed by Ikeda Shinkai and dated 1678, this sword was donation to the shrine by [[Ikeda Tsunamasa]], It is now kept at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6336 |title=太刀〈銘（菊紋）井上真改／延宝五年八月日〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=20 August 2020}} ==Gallery== Kibitsuhiko jinja -01.jpg|Panorama File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja honden.JPG|Honden（Okayama P.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja zuishinmon.JPG|Zuishin-mon（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko Jinja 07.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern（Okayama C.ICP） File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja Heian-sugi.JPG|Peace Sugi File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja torii.JPG|Torii File:Kibitsuhiko-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsuhiko-jinja}} *[https://www.kibitsuhiko.or.jp Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Bizen Province]] [[Category:Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kibi clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kibitsuhiko Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]]
